items.title: Memory SQLite should use WAL journal mode by default

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Problem
Memory index SQLite database (`~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite`) is created with `journal_mode=delete` (SQLite default). This causes frequent database corruption when the gateway receives SIGTERM during write operations (restart, update, config changes).

## Impact
- Users see "database disk image is malformed" errors
- Memory search stops working until manual `rm + openclaw memory index`
- Happens repeatedly since gateway restarts are common

## Root Cause
`journal_mode=delete` removes the journal file after each transaction. If the process is killed between deleting the journal and completing the write, the database corrupts.

## Suggested Fix
Set `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;` when creating or opening the memory database. WAL mode:
- Is crash-safe (survives SIGTERM/SIGKILL mid-write)
- Allows concurrent reads during writes
- Is the recommended mode for applications that restart frequently

```js
// After opening the database connection:
db.pragma('journal_mode = WAL');
```

## Workaround
```bash
sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite "PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;"
```
But this resets if the DB is recreated (e.g., after `openclaw memory index` on a corrupted DB).

## Environment
- OpenClaw 2026.3.2
- macOS arm64
- SQLite via better-sqlite3

items.title: Feishu: block-only turns can drop replies (dispatch ends with replies=0)

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Problem

Feishu DM inbound can be processed successfully by the gateway/agent, but no reply is delivered to the Feishu client when the turn emits only `block` payloads and no `final` payload.

Symptoms observed in production:

- Inbound DM is logged and routed.
- Agent session contains assistant text.
- Feishu dispatch log shows `queuedFinal=false, replies=0`.
- Feishu client receives nothing.

## Root Cause

In `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts`, `deliver()` returns early for `info.kind === "block"` unless `(streamingEnabled && useCard)`.

So for non-card block chunks (or block-only turns), text/media are dropped entirely if no final payload follows.

## Expected

If no final payload arrives, block payloads should still be delivered at idle as a fallback.

## Fix proposal

Buffer dropped block payloads (text/media) and flush on `onIdle` when no final payload was seen in the turn.

## PR

Opened PR with fix and test:

- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/36032

items.title: Health-monitor falsely detects Discord connections as 'stuck' during LLM inference, causing restart thrash and 'Gateway is draining' errors

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Description

Health-monitor detects Discord channel connections as "stuck" and repeatedly restarts them, causing `Gateway is draining` errors for agents attempting to send messages during the restart window. This creates a cycle where connections are restarted every ~10 minutes.

## Environment

- **OpenClaw version**: 2026.3.2 (latest stable)
- **OS**: macOS (arm64) + Ubuntu (Hetzner VPS, 503GB RAM)
- **Setup**: Multi-agent gateway with 4 Discord bots (macOS) / 10 Discord bots (Ubuntu)
- **Channels**: Discord + Telegram per agent

## Symptoms

### 1. Health-monitor restart thrashing

Every ~10 minutes, health-monitor restarts Discord connections with `reason: stuck`:

```
[health-monitor] [discord:livius] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
[health-monitor] [discord:artemis] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
[health-monitor] [discord:onyx] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
```

Telegram connections also restart with `reason: stale-socket` at similar intervals.

**Scale (4-hour window):**

| Host | Health-monitor restarts | Slow listeners |
|------|------------------------|----------------|
| macOS (4 bots) | ~15 | ~4 |
| Ubuntu (10 bots) | **145** | **1,025** |

### 2. Root cause: DiscordMessageListener blocks event queue

A single `MESSAGE_CREATE` event blocks the listener for **minutes**:

```
[discord/monitor] Slow listener detected: DiscordMessageListener took 483517ms (483.5 seconds) for MESSAGE_CREATE
[discord/monitor] Slow listener detected: DiscordMessageListener took 48153ms (48.2 seconds) for MESSAGE_CREATE
```

This appears to happen when an agent processes an LLM inference synchronously within the Discord message listener, blocking the entire event queue. While the inference runs, health-monitor sees no activity and marks the connection as "stuck."

### 3. DiscordReactionListener slow warnings

Additionally, `DiscordReactionListener` and `DiscordReactionRemoveListener` regularly exceed 1-2.4 seconds:

```
[EventQueue] Slow listener detected: DiscordReactionListener took 2398ms for MESSAGE_REACTION_ADD
[EventQueue] Slow listener detected: DiscordReactionRemoveListener took 1580ms for MESSAGE_REACTION_REMOVE
```

On the Ubuntu host, 1,025 slow listener warnings in 4 hours (~4 per minute).

### 4. Agent impact: "Gateway is draining"

When health-monitor restarts a connection, any agent trying to use that Discord connection gets:

```
Gateway is draining, please try again later
```

This disrupts agent workflows mid-task.

## Expected behavior

1. LLM inference within Discord message handling should not block the event queue (async processing)
2. Health-monitor should distinguish between "connection dead" and "connection busy processing a long-running request"
3. The grace window added in v2026.3.2 (#32367) helps but doesn't fully solve the issue when inference takes 48-483 seconds

## Possible solutions

1. **Process LLM inference asynchronously** - decouple the Discord event listener from the LLM response pipeline so the event queue stays responsive
2. **Extend health-monitor awareness** - if a session is actively processing (LLM inference in progress), don't mark the connection as stuck
3. **Configurable stuck timeout** - allow operators to set a longer threshold for multi-agent setups where long inferences are expected

## Additional context

- The v2026.3.2 changelog mentions: "prevent health-monitor restart thrash for channels that just (re)started by adding a per-channel startup-connect grace window" (#32367) - this helps post-restart but doesn't prevent the initial "stuck" detection during long inferences
- Both hosts run the same version (2026.3.2) with identical behavior
- 56 zombie processes (defunct) accumulate on the Ubuntu host from repeated restarts (oldest from Feb 27)
- Potentially related: the zombie process accumulation suggests the restart flow doesn't fully clean up child processes

items.title: Telegram media group fetch intermittently fails on Node.js 25 (undici IPv6)

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Description

Intermittent `TypeError: fetch failed` when downloading photos from Telegram media groups (multiple photos sent at once). Single photo messages work fine.

## Environment
- **OpenClaw**: 2026.3.1 (stable)
- **Node.js**: 25.6.1 (arm64, macOS 26.3)
- **OS**: macOS Sequoia 26.3 (Apple Silicon)

## Error
```
[telegram] media group: skipping photo that failed to fetch: MediaFetchError: Failed to fetch media from https://api.telegram.org/file/bot<token>/photos/file_XXXX.jpg: TypeError: fetch failed
```

## Frequency
- 5 failures across 8 days (Feb 26 – Mar 4)
- Only happens with media groups (multiple photos), never single photo messages
- 1 out of N photos in a group fails, rest download fine

## Root cause suspicion
Node.js 25 undici HTTP client + IPv6 resolution. When multiple concurrent fetches hit `api.telegram.org`, one occasionally fails due to IPv6/IPv4 race condition.

## Workarounds applied (partial fix)
1. `/etc/hosts`: `149.154.166.110 api.telegram.org` (force IPv4)
2. `NODE_OPTIONS=--dns-result-order=ipv4first` in LaunchAgent plist
3. Patched all `ssrf-*.js` files: added `family: 4` to dispatcher options
4. These reduced frequency but didn't eliminate it completely

## Related
- #24854 (IPv6 media download bug)

## Suggestion
Consider adding a retry mechanism for media group photo downloads — if one fetch fails, retry once before skipping. This would eliminate the user-visible error for intermittent network issues.

labels.name: stale

items.body:
# Incident Record: Intermittent `Invalid diff: now finding less tool calls!` (Max/Jason)

- Date: 2026-03-05 (Asia/Taipei)
- Reporter: Marcus
- Impact: Intermittent task failures in Max/Jason paths; noisy/error outputs despite successful runs before/after.

## Symptom
- Error string:
  - `Invalid diff: now finding less tool calls!`
- Seen mainly in:
  - Max and Jason execution paths
  - Cron and DM-related execution contexts

## Confirmed Evidence
- Max cron job (new job without model override):
  - Job: `707ec10b-c6bc-4f8c-9bbb-46d6df8dd037`
  - 17:00 run failed with:
    - status: `error`
    - summary/error: `Invalid diff: now finding less tool calls!`
    - sessionKey: `agent:max:cron:707ec10b-c6bc-4f8c-9bbb-46d6df8dd037:run:9a50c04c-af50-436d-b6f0-1e4f057ba625`
  - Neighbor runs (16:30 / 17:30 / 18:00 / 18:30): `ok`
- System log correlation:
  - Repeated Discord channel health monitor restarts for `discord:max` (reason `stuck`)
  - Repeated slow listener signals (`DiscordMessageListener` large durations)

## Important Context
- Old cron jobs using `payload.model=toolbox/qwen3-coder` were migrated and disabled.
- New replacement jobs were created without explicit `payload.model` override.
- Error still appears intermittently after migration.

## Hypothesis
Likely runtime/tool-call diff instability under specific conditions (local model + tool-calling path + event/listener pressure), not merely user cron misconfiguration.

## Mitigations Applied
1. Migrated cron jobs away from hardcoded invalid model override.
2. Restarted gateway and executed smoke runs (successful immediately after restart).
3. Replaced noisy Max calendar job with shorter prompt + fresh session key:
   - New job: `e0eb72c1-735d-4ff8-9426-37eddacd9d65`
   - Old job disabled.
4. Added monitor job for alert only on consecutive failures:
   - `fcf20a5d-f1fd-4b90-8b69-287b3d02828d`

## Repro Clues for Maintainers
- Intermittent nature: fails one run, succeeds surrounding runs.
- Seen on Max/Jason paths more than Ruby/David.
- Co-occurs with Discord `stuck` restarts and listener slowdown.

## Suggested Next Diagnostics (upstream)
- Add richer tracing around tool-call diff application when model output changes tool-call count between partial/final chunks.
- Correlate per-run model response chunks with event-loop lag and listener durations.
- Validate handling for local model provider outputs that may emit unstable/incomplete tool-call structures.

labels.name: stale

items.body:
## Summary
Long-running agent sessions cause the Telegram provider to enter a stale-socket → restart → re-deliver loop, sending the same message to the user every 10 minutes indefinitely.

## Environment
- OpenClaw `2026.3.2`
- macOS (Darwin 24.6.0, Apple Silicon)
- Node v22.22.0
- Telegram channel enabled, `dmPolicy: "pairing"`

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run multiple concurrent agents (6 workstation agents) with a long-running main agent session
2. Main agent completes a turn and sends a Telegram message
3. Wait ~30 minutes

## Observed Behavior
- `health-monitor` detects `stale-socket` and restarts the Telegram provider every ~30 minutes
- Each restart re-delivers the last queued message from the completed agent turn
- The same message repeats every 10 minutes indefinitely until the gateway is manually restarted
- Gateway log shows: `[health-monitor] [telegram:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stale-socket)` followed by the same agent response re-sent

```
2026-03-05T04:57:08.482Z [health-monitor] [telegram:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stale-socket)
2026-03-05T04:57:10.851Z [telegram] [default] starting provider (@WhitneyAIOCBot)
2026-03-04T21:04:26.422-08:00 Ledger is building. All six agents now in progress...
NO_REPLY
2026-03-05T05:27:08.525Z [health-monitor] [telegram:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stale-socket)
2026-03-05T05:27:09.558Z [telegram] [default] starting provider (@WhitneyAIOCBot)
```

This pattern repeated for 6+ hours until the gateway was manually restarted.

## Expected Behavior
- Once a message is successfully delivered, it should be marked as delivered and not re-queued on provider restart
- Stale-socket recovery should not trigger re-delivery of already-sent messages
- Idempotent delivery: if `sendMessage ok` was logged for a message, it should never be re-sent

## Workaround
`launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u)/ai.openclaw.gateway` to restart the gateway and clear the stuck session state.

items.title: Config migration issue: Invalid keys from v2026.3.2 cause gateway startup failure in v2026.3.3

labels.name: stale

items.body:
## Description

Gateway fails to start after upgrading from v2026.3.2 to v2026.3.3 due to config schema mismatch. Keys that were valid in v2026.3.2 (`autoApproveDevices`, `requirePairing`) are rejected as invalid in v2026.3.3, causing 18+ hours of downtime.

## Environment

- **OpenClaw versions**: 2026.3.2 → 2026.3.3
- **Platform**: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0)
- **Gateway mode**: local, bind=lan
- **Affected services**: Discord bot, Telegram bot, node connections

## Timeline of Events

1. **March 3, 22:39** - Running v2026.3.2, config normal
2. **March 4, 08:11** - Auto-update to v2026.3.2, `lastTouchedVersion` updated
3. **March 4, 23:30** - Config reload attempted, validation failure detected
   ```
   gateway.controlUi: Unrecognized keys: "autoApproveDevices", "requirePairing"
   ```
4. **March 5, 00:00** - Gateway restart triggered (automatic/scheduled)
5. **March 5, 00:00-18:40** - Gateway repeatedly fails to start (config validation error)
6. **March 5, 18:40** - Manual intervention: removed invalid keys, gateway started successfully

## Error Messages

From logs (`/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-03-05.log`):

```json
{
  "0": "  - gateway.controlUi: Unrecognized keys: \"autoApproveDevices\", \"requirePairing\"",
  "_meta": {
    "logLevelName": "ERROR",
    "date": "2026-03-04T15:00:00.666Z"
  },
  "time": "2026-03-05T00:00:00.666+09:00"
}
```

This error repeated continuously from 00:00 to 18:40 (18 hours 40 minutes).

## Config Section Affected

```json
{
  "gateway": {
    "controlUi": {
      "allowedOrigins": [...],
      "autoApproveDevices": true,    // ← Invalid in v2026.3.3
      "requirePairing": false         // ← Invalid in v2026.3.3
    }
  }
}
```

## Impact

- **Gateway**: Unable to start for 18+ hours
- **Discord bot**: Offline during entire period
- **Telegram bot**: Offline during entire period
- **Connected nodes**: Unable to connect or communicate
- **User experience**: Complete service outage

## Root Cause Analysis

1. v2026.3.2 added `autoApproveDevices` and `requirePairing` to config schema
2. v2026.3.3 removed or deprecated these keys without migration
3. Config file retained v2026.3.2 keys after update
4. Gateway startup validation rejects these keys in v2026.3.3
5. No automatic config migration or cleanup occurred

## Expected Behavior

One of the following should happen during version upgrades:

1. **Automatic migration**: Remove deprecated keys from config
2. **Graceful deprecation**: Warn about invalid keys but continue startup
3. **Migration script**: Provide `openclaw config migrate` command
4. **Clear documentation**: Breaking changes in release notes with migration guide

## Actual Behavior

- Gateway silently fails to start
- Only error is buried in logs
- No user notification or recovery guidance
- Manual intervention required to identify and fix issue

## Workaround

Manually edit `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` and remove the invalid keys:

```bash
# Remove these lines from gateway.controlUi section:
"autoApproveDevices": true,
"requirePairing": false
```

Then restart gateway:
```bash
openclaw gateway restart
```

## Suggested Fixes

### Short-term (patch release)
1. Add config migration for v2026.3.2 → v2026.3.3 transition
2. Document breaking changes in release notes
3. Add startup warning when unknown keys are detected (instead of hard failure)

### Long-term
1. Implement config schema versioning
2. Automatic migration system for config updates
3. Validate config on save (in Control UI) to prevent invalid keys from being written
4. Better error messages pointing to specific config issues and solutions
5. `openclaw config validate` command to check config before restart

## Additional Context

- Config metadata shows `lastTouchedVersion: "2026.3.2"` but running v2026.3.3
- Gateway has hot-reload functionality but validation prevents it from working
- No backup config system to automatically rollback on failure
- Logs show repeated startup attempts but no clear indication of how to fix

## Related

- Issue #29385 (mentioned in logs regarding `allowedOrigins` seeding)

---

This issue caused significant downtime and could be prevented with better config migration handling. Happy to provide more details or test fixes.

### Summary
Implement an ephemeral status message for Telegram reasoning/thinking streaming to replace the current prepended/appended text blocks.

### Problem
Currently, with `reasoning: stream` enabled on Telegram, the reasoning blocks are prepended/appended to the message as it generates. This leaves a large "wall of text" that persists even after the final response is delivered, cluttering the chat history.

### Proposed Solution
1. **Initial Status**: When the model starts thinking, send a small status message (e.g., `🐻 Batou is thinking...`).
2. **Live Updates**: As reasoning tokens arrive, update (edit) this status message with short snippets of the reasoning process.
3. **Clean Handover**: Once the final response is ready, delete the status message and send the final response as a clean, new message.

### Why this matters
This provides the "live" feeling of a thinking agent without the permanent clutter in the chat UI. It makes the Telegram interface feel more professional and "slick".

- **Gateway:** systemd user service

2. Restart gateway: `systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway.service`
3. Check version: `openclaw status`

## Expected

```
Gateway: ... app 2026.3.2 ...
```

## Actual

```
Gateway: ... app 2026.2.24 ...
```

## Verification

The npm package is correctly v2026.3.2:

```bash
$ npm ls -g openclaw --depth=0
└── openclaw@2026.3.2

$ node -e "console.log(require('/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/package.json').version)"
2026.3.2

$ openclaw --version
2026.3.2
```

The gateway binary (`dist/entry.js`) was updated (timestamp matches reinstall), and `package.json` reads `2026.3.2`. The version string reported by the gateway via WebSocket RPC handshake appears to be a stale build-time constant that wasn't bumped in the 2026.3.2 release.

## Impact

Cosmetic only — all v2026.3.2 features and fixes are functional. The dashboard and `openclaw status` just display the wrong version number.

## Additional Context

- Multiple gateway restarts and full `npm install -g openclaw@latest` reinstall did not resolve it
- Custom control-ui (via `gateway.controlUi.root`) is in use but the version string comes from the gateway RPC probe, not the UI bundle

items.title: bug: openclaw doctor silently strips unknown config keys without warning

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Bug Report

### Summary
`openclaw doctor` silently removes unrecognised keys from `openclaw.json` during its repair pass without prompting the user or logging a clear warning. For a tool managing production-level agent configs, silent data loss is dangerous — users may not notice that intentional (or future-compatible) config entries have been removed.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Add any key to `openclaw.json` that is not present in the current config schema (e.g. `agents.defaults.pdfModel`)
2. Run `openclaw doctor --non-interactive`
3. Doctor completes successfully with no visible warning
4. The unrecognised key is silently removed from the config file

### Expected Behaviour
Doctor should:
- **Warn** the user that an unknown key was found: e.g. `⚠️ Unknown config key: agents.defaults.pdfModel — not in schema`
- In interactive mode: **ask for confirmation** before removing it
- In non-interactive mode (`--non-interactive`): **log the removal** clearly so it appears in output/logs
- Optionally: preserve unknown keys with a comment/flag rather than deleting them (forward-compatibility)

### Actual Behaviour
- Doctor removes the key with no warning, no prompt, no log entry
- The user only discovers the loss by diffing the config backup (`openclaw.json.bak`) against the current file

### Notes
- A `.bak` file **is** created before changes, which is good — but this is not communicated to the user
- The silent strip is particularly risky when users are trying to configure features documented in newer versions that haven't landed in the schema yet

### Environment
- OpenClaw: v2026.3.2
- OS: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0, arm64)
- Node: v24.13.0

### Impact
**Medium** — data loss from config without user awareness. Low blast radius (config only) but erodes trust and makes debugging very difficult.

- acpx: 0.1.15 (plugin-local)

- Codex ACP: 0.9.5 (gpt-5.3-codex)

## Symptoms
Every `sessions_spawn(runtime="acp", agentId="codex")` fails with:
```
AcpRuntimeError: acpx exited with code 5
code=ACP_TURN_FAILED
```

Sessions are created and immediately marked `[closed]`.

## Reproduction
```js
sessions_spawn({
  runtime: "acp",
  agentId: "codex",
  mode: "run",
  task: "Say hello"
})
// → AcpRuntimeError: acpx exited with code 5
```

## What works
The exact same acpx binary, same flags, same auth, works perfectly via CLI:
```bash
ACPX_CMD="./extensions/acpx/node_modules/.bin/acpx"
$ACPX_CMD --format json --json-strict --cwd /path --approve-all \
  --non-interactive-permissions deny --ttl 5 \
  codex sessions ensure --name test
# ✅ Session created

echo "Say hello" | $ACPX_CMD --format json --json-strict --cwd /path \
  --approve-all --non-interactive-permissions deny --ttl 5 \
  codex prompt --session test --file -
# ✅ Full response with tool calls
```

## Investigation
- Auth (OPENAI_API_KEY) is valid and working
- Plugin config: `permissionMode: "approve-all"`, enabled: true
- The acpx `ensure` + `prompt` flow works identically to what runtime.ts does
- Bug appears to be in the Gateway ACP session lifecycle (ensure → prompt → result collection)
- Exit code alternates between 4 and 5 depending on session state
- `session/load` returns "Resource not found" (expected for new sessions, acpx handles this by creating new), but the Gateway may be treating this as fatal

## Workaround
Direct acpx CLI ("telephone game") works fine as a bypass.

## Config
```json
{
  "acp": {
    "backend": "acpx",
    "defaultAgent": "codex",
    "allowedAgents": ["pi", "claude", "codex", "opencode", "gemini", "claude-code", "codeclaw"]
```

## Feature Gap

### Context
OpenClaw v2026.3.2 ships a native `pdf` tool that reads PDFs via Anthropic's native PDF API. The docs state the tool registers when OpenClaw can resolve a PDF-capable model config via:

1. `agents.defaults.pdfModel`
2. Fallback to `agents.defaults.imageModel`
3. Fallback to best-effort provider defaults

### Problem
`agents.defaults.pdfModel` is **not present in the config schema**, so `config.patch` and `config.apply` both reject it with a validation error. Attempting to add it directly to `openclaw.json` (bypassing schema) causes the gateway to malfunction on restart, requiring `openclaw doctor` to clean up.

Meanwhile, `agents.defaults.imageModel` **is** in the schema and can be set — but using it as a fallback to trigger the `pdf` tool doesn't appear to work in practice (the tool is not registered in the agent's session even after setting `imageModel` and restarting).

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Install OpenClaw v2026.3.2
2. Run `openclaw config patch '{ "agents": { "defaults": { "pdfModel": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6" } } }'`
3. Observe: schema validation error — `pdfModel` is not a recognised key

### Expected Behaviour
- `pdfModel` should be a valid, documented config key under `agents.defaults`
- Setting either `pdfModel` or `imageModel` to an Anthropic model should reliably register the `pdf` tool in the agent's session
- `openclaw doctor` should warn rather than silently strip unknown keys

### Environment
- OpenClaw: v2026.3.2
- Provider: Anthropic (claude-sonnet-4-6)
- OS: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0, arm64)
- Node: v24.13.0

The `pdf` tool is advertised as a v2026.3.2 feature but is effectively inaccessible without a valid config path to enable it.

items.title: feat: Context Budget Awareness — Working Buffer for Long Sessions

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Summary

Long-running sessions (especially coding sessions via backup proxy) can exhaust the context window, leading to:
- **Persona drift** — system prompt rules get squeezed out
- **Tool hallucination** — tool schema details are lost
- **Memory fragmentation** — earlier conversation content is dropped

Currently OpenClaw mitigates this with short sessions + memory persistence, but there's measurable room for improvement in long-session scenarios.

## Proposal

Implement a **gateway-level context budget awareness system** with graduated intervention:

| Phase | What | Timeline |
|-------|------|----------|
| **Phase 0** | **Observe only** — track per-turn token usage ratio, log to session metadata | 1–2 weeks |
| **Phase 1** | **Soft hint** — inject `[context-budget: 62% used, 76k remaining]` when usage ≥ soft threshold (~60%) | 2–3 days dev + 1 week observe |
| **Phase 2** | **Hard compact + snapshot** — auto-summarize + produce a structured working-buffer snapshot when ≥ hard threshold (~78%) | 1 week dev + 2 weeks observe |
| **Phase 3** | **Truncation recovery** — auto-inject last snapshot into new/restarted sessions | 3–5 days dev |

### Architecture Principle

> **Sensing at Gateway, Strategy at Agent.**

The agent cannot introspect its own context usage — token counts are runtime-layer information. The gateway must compute and inject hints; the agent reacts with behavioral adjustments.

## Working Buffer Snapshot Schema (Phase 2)

```json
{
  "sessionKey": "abc123",
  "version": 1,
  "updatedAt": "2026-03-05T17:00:00+08:00",
  "turn": 42,
  "usage_pct": 78,
  "objective": "Current task description",
  "decisions": ["Chose approach B for performance", "Rejected C for compatibility"],
  "open_loops": ["Feature A 80% done, needs tests", "Awaiting user confirmation on UI"],
  "tool_state": [
    {"tool": "exec", "status": "ok", "note": "git push succeeded"},
    {"tool": "write", "status": "pending", "note": "config.yaml pending"}
  ],
  "constraints": ["PHI compliance", "B5 format"],
  "next_actions": ["Complete feature A", "Run tests", "Update docs"],
  "source_window": {"fromTurn": 120, "toTurn": 168}
}
```

## Proposed Config

```yaml
contextBudget:
  enabled: true           # master toggle
  soft: 0.60              # inject hint
  hard: 0.78              # trigger compact + snapshot
  perAgent:               # per-agent override
    main: true
    cron: false
    heartbeat: false
    subagent: "inherit"
```

Config path: `gateway.contextBudget` (new section in config schema)

## Success Metrics (KPIs)

| Metric | Measurement | Target |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| Tool failure rate | Tool hallucination/error rate in high-context (>60%) sessions | >30% decrease |
| Persona drift | System prompt adherence deviation events | >50% decrease |
| Recovery first-turn corrections | Extra corrections needed after compact/truncation | ≤1 |
| Token overhead | compact + hint + snapshot cost | <5–8% |

Phase 0 establishes the baseline; Phase 1+ compares against it.

## Gateway Changes Required

1. **Session token tracking** — record per-turn usage in session middleware
2. **Hint injection** — inject budget hint during message composition
3. **Compact trigger** — new compact logic (call haiku for summarization)
4. **Config schema** — add `contextBudget` config block
5. **Working buffer I/O** — snapshot read/write to `memory/working-buffer-{session_id}.json`

## Rollback Plan

1. Set `contextBudget.enabled: false` → gateway restart → all behavior reverts
2. Existing `memory/working-buffer-*.json` snapshots retained for debug (auto-cleanup after 7 days)
3. Phase 2 compact is irreversible (summarized conversations can't be un-summarized), but snapshots include `source_window` for traceability
4. Emergency: single flag toggle + restart

## Background

- Originated from #coding-team discussion (2026-03-05) analyzing proactive agent design patterns
- Reference skill: `halthelobster/proactive-agent` (not installed, concept only)
- Cross-referenced with LangGraph / AutoGen long-conversation management strategies
- Full spec: `documents/working-buffer-plan.md` in workspace

items.title: [cron] Announce delivery uses hallucinated recipient IDs instead of configured delivery.to

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
---
title: "OpenClaw Cron Delivery Bug Analysis"
date: 2026-03-05
tags: [openclaw, bug, delivery, feishu, cron]
source: "本地服务器 OpenClaw 2026.3.2 实例排查"
---

# OpenClaw Cron Delivery: Agent 可覆盖配置中的收件人 ID

## 问题记录

### 现象

服务器上运行 OpenClaw 2026.3.2，配置了多个 cron 定时任务（每日技术简报、工作总结、EvoMap 任务等），delivery 配置中明确指定了飞书收件人：

```json
"delivery": {
  "mode": "announce",
  "channel": "feishu",
  "to": "ou_fa4442c31fcc86f13b3b2bba11d0d9eb"
}
```

但 delivery-queue 中积压了 5 条失败消息，全部报错 `Request failed with status code 400`。检查发现大部分消息的 `to` 字段被替换为不存在的飞书用户 ID：

| 消息 | 配置的 to | 实际投递的 to | 结果 |
|------|-----------|---------------|------|
| EvoMap 日报 2/26 | ou_fa4442c... | ou_5e9a9a1a1a1a... | 400 |
| EvoMap 日报 2/25 | ou_fa4442c... | ou_24f62e1b7095... | 400 |
| NOVA 记忆维护报告 | ou_fa4442c... | ou_3e91e5e07858... | 400 |
| 技术简报 2/18 | ou_fa4442c... | ou_fa4442c31fcc... | 400 |
| EvoMap 日报 3/1 | ou_fa4442c... | ou_3f5f7e1f27c5... | 400 |

### 根因分析

通过源码分析（基于 openclaw@2026.3.2），问题链路如下：

#### 1. Cron Delivery Plan 解析（正常）

`src/cron/delivery.ts` 中 `resolveCronDeliveryPlan()` 正确提取 `delivery.to`：

```typescript
const deliveryTo = normalizeTo((delivery as { to?: unknown })?.to);
const payloadTo = normalizeTo(payload?.to);
const to = deliveryTo ?? payloadTo;
```

优先使用 `delivery.to`，逻辑正确。

#### 2. Delivery Target 解析（正常）

`src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.ts` 中 `resolveDeliveryTarget()` 将 `explicitTo` 正确传递到 `resolveSessionDeliveryTarget()`，显式目标优先于 session 中的 `lastTo`。

#### 3. Announce 流程中的收件人覆盖（问题所在）

`src/agents/subagent-announce.ts` 中 `resolveAnnounceOrigin()` 函数：

```typescript
function resolveAnnounceOrigin(
  entry?: DeliveryContextSessionSource,
  requesterOrigin?: DeliveryContext,
): DeliveryContext | undefined {
  const normalizedRequester = normalizeDeliveryContext(requesterOrigin);
  // ...
  return mergeDeliveryContext(boundOrigin, requesterOrigin);
}
```

在 announce 流程中，系统调用 `callGateway()` 让 LLM Agent 将 cron 输出"转化为用户友好的消息"。这个过程中：

1. Agent（MiniMax M2.5）在生成回复时**幻觉出了假的飞书用户 ID**
2. 幻觉的 ID 通过 session 状态（`lastTo`）或 delivery context 合并被写入
3. 后续投递使用了被污染的 `to` 字段

#### 4. 飞书端缺少前置校验

`extensions/feishu/src/targets.ts` 中 `normalizeFeishuTarget()` 对任何字符串都直接放行，`resolveReceiveIdType()` 只做格式判断（`ou_` 前缀 → `open_id`），不校验 ID 是否真实存在。飞书 API 收到不存在的用户 ID 后返回 400。

### 影响范围

- 所有使用 `announce` 模式的 cron delivery
- 特别是当 Agent 模型能力较弱（如 MiniMax M2.5）时，幻觉概率更高
- 消息进入 delivery-queue 后最多重试 5 次，跨天仍然失败

### 建议修复方向

1. **在 cron announce 流程中锁定 `to` 字段** — delivery config 中指定的 `to` 应作为 immutable 参数，不允许被 announce 流程中的 session/LLM 输出覆盖
2. **添加飞书用户 ID 预验证** — 在入队前调用飞书 API 验证用户 ID 是否存在，对 400 错误不做重试
3. **delivery-queue 对 4xx 错误实施断路器** — 区分临时性 5xx 和永久性 4xx，4xx 直接标记为 failed 不再重试

### 相关代码文件

- `src/cron/delivery.ts` — Cron delivery plan 解析
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.ts` — Delivery target 解析
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` — Cron 隔离 agent 运行主逻辑
- `src/agents/subagent-announce.ts` — Announce 子流程
- `src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.ts` — Announce 投递分发
- `src/utils/delivery-context.ts` — DeliveryContext 合并逻辑
- `extensions/feishu/src/targets.ts` — 飞书 ID 解析与校验

---

## GitHub Issue 内容

以下为准备提交到 openclaw/openclaw 的 Issue 正文：

---

### Title: `[cron] Announce delivery uses hallucinated recipient IDs instead of configured delivery.to`

### Body:

**OpenClaw version:** 2026.3.2
**Channel:** Feishu
**Model:** MiniMax M2.5

## Description

When a cron job is configured with an explicit `delivery.to` recipient (Feishu open_id), the announce delivery flow sometimes delivers messages to **wrong, non-existent recipient IDs** instead of the configured target. These appear to be hallucinated by the LLM agent during the announce formatting step.

## Reproduction

1. Configure a cron job with explicit delivery target:

```json
{
  "delivery": {
    "mode": "announce",
    "channel": "feishu",
    "to": "ou_fa4442c31fcc86f13b3b2bba11d0d9eb"
  }
}
```

2. Let the cron job run (e.g., daily tech digest, EvoMap task report)
3. Check `delivery-queue/*.json` — some messages have a different `to` field than configured

## Observed behavior

Delivery queue contains messages with hallucinated recipient IDs:

```
Configured:  ou_fa4442c31fcc86f13b3b2bba11d0d9eb
Actual:      ou_5e9a9a1a1a1a1a1a1a1a1a1a1a1a1a1a  ← hallucinated
             ou_3e91e5e07858c53d22e94a81b40d1beb  ← hallucinated
             ou_3f5f7e1f27c5f0f1af8fce97a2a1c52a  ← hallucinated
```

All fail with `Request failed with status code 400` from Feishu API and retry 4-5 times before giving up.

## Expected behavior

The `delivery.to` field from the cron job config should be treated as the authoritative recipient. The announce flow should not allow the LLM agent to override or replace this value.

## Root cause analysis

The issue appears to be in the announce flow (`subagent-announce.ts`). When `resolveAnnounceOrigin()` merges delivery context, session-derived or agent-output values can override the explicit `to` from the cron delivery config:

```typescript
// subagent-announce.ts
origin: mergeDeliveryContext(boundOrigin, requesterOrigin),
```

The `mergeDeliveryContext()` function in `utils/delivery-context.ts` uses a primary/fallback pattern, but the session state (`lastTo`) can become contaminated when the LLM agent hallucinates recipient information during the announce formatting step.

Additionally, `extensions/feishu/src/targets.ts` performs no existence validation on recipient IDs — any string matching `ou_*` is accepted as a valid `open_id`.

## Suggested fixes

1. **Pin `to` for cron announce delivery** — When `delivery.to` is explicitly set in cron config, pass it as an immutable override that cannot be replaced during the announce flow
2. **Add permanent error classification for 400s** — The `PERMANENT_ANNOUNCE_DELIVERY_ERROR_PATTERNS` in `subagent-announce.ts` already handles some cases; adding a pattern for invalid recipient (HTTP 400 from Feishu) would prevent futile retries
3. **Optional: Pre-validate Feishu recipient IDs** before enqueuing

## Related

- #27898 (delivery target resolution failure handling)
- `requireExplicitMessageTarget` logic in `cron/isolated-agent/run.ts:528`

## Environment

- OS: OpenCloudOS 9 (Linux 6.6.117 x86_64)
- Runtime: Node v22.22.0
- Model: MiniMax M2.5 via Anthropic Messages API
- Channel: Feishu (2 accounts: default + suger)
- 3 agents: main, tiger, suger with multiple cron jobs

items.title: Feature: Add session delete commands (rm/clear) without full reset
items.body:
### Problem
`openclaw sessions` can list sessions and `sessions cleanup` can prune by policy, but there is no direct way to intentionally delete sessions.

Current options are either:
1. `openclaw reset` (too destructive), or
2. manually deleting files under `~/.openclaw/agents/*/sessions/` (unsafe and unintuitive).

### Why this matters
Users need a normal CLI workflow to clear stale/test sessions without resetting full config/auth.

### Requested commands
- `openclaw sessions rm <key>`
- `openclaw sessions clear --all`
- `openclaw sessions clear --older-than <duration>`
- optional: `--agent <id>`, `--all-agents`, `--dry-run`, `--json`

### Expected behavior
- Remove session entry + transcript safely.
- Keep store consistency (no orphaned pointers).
- Return deleted count and affected keys.

### Current version
- OpenClaw `2026.3.2`
- Repro:
  - `openclaw sessions` (lists sessions)
  - `openclaw sessions cleanup --enforce` (does not fully delete intended sessions)
  - no direct delete command available

items.title: [Feature] Enable Playwright navigation interception for local-managed browser mode
items.body:
## Summary

Local-managed browser mode should use Playwright for navigation interception, just like remote-cdp mode does. This would enable redirect-hop inspection for SSRF policy enforcement, making the browser tool more secure and functional.

## Background

Currently, browser profile modes have different capabilities for navigation interception:

| Mode | `usesPersistentPlaywright` | Redirect Inspection |
|------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Chrome MCP (`existing-session`) | `false` | ❌ None |
| Remote CDP | `true` | ✅ Playwright route handler |
| Local-managed | `false` | ❌ None |

The decision is based solely on `cdpIsLoopback`:

```typescript
// extensions/browser/src/browser/profile-capabilities.ts
if (!profile.cdpIsLoopback) {
  return { usesPersistentPlaywright: true };  // remote-cdp
}
return { usesPersistentPlaywright: false };   // local-managed
```

## Problem

### 1. Local-managed has higher SSRF risk but weaker protection

SSRF attacks are **more dangerous** in local-managed mode:
- OpenClaw and Chrome run on the same machine
- That machine typically has access to internal networks (databases, admin panels, internal APIs)
- SSRF attacks can directly access these internal resources

Remote-cdp mode actually has **lower** SSRF risk:
- Chrome runs on a remote machine
- SSRF attacks affect the remote machine's network, not OpenClaw's internal network

Yet the current design gives Playwright protection to remote-cdp (lower risk) but not to local-managed (higher risk).

### 2. `playwright-core` is already a dependency

OpenClaw already includes `playwright-core` as a dependency:

```json
// package.json
"playwright-core": "1.59.1"
```

`playwright-core` can connect to local Chrome via CDP:

```typescript
await chromium.connectOverCDP("ws://localhost:9222/...");
```

There's no technical barrier or additional dependency required.

### 3. Current workaround is broken

When `dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork: false` is configured:
- Hostname URLs are blocked (expected, needs redirect inspection)
- IP literal URLs are also blocked due to a bug in `requiresInspectableBrowserNavigationRedirectsForUrl` (#66109, #66268)

This makes local-managed mode completely unusable with strict SSRF policy.

### 4. Related issues

- #66109 - Browser SSRF Policy blocks all actions on hostname URLs
- #66268 - SSRF policy blocks hostname-based navigation even with profile=user
- #45153 - Sandbox browser ignores top-level browser.ssrfPolicy
- #57055 - Sandbox browser ignores global browser.ssrfPolicy configuration

## Proposed Solution

Enable Playwright navigation for local-managed mode:

```typescript
// Option 1: Always use Playwright for navigation
if (profile.driver === "existing-session") {
  return { usesPersistentPlaywright: false };  // Chrome MCP uses its own mechanism
}
return { usesPersistentPlaywright: true };     // Both local-managed and remote-cdp

// Option 2: Make it configurable
return { 
  usesPersistentPlaywright: profile.usePlaywright ?? !profile.cdpIsLoopback 
};
```

This would allow `gotoPageWithNavigationGuard` to intercept all navigation requests and check each redirect against SSRF policy.

## Benefits

1. **Better security**: Local-managed mode gets the same redirect-hop inspection as remote-cdp
2. **Consistent behavior**: All modes handle SSRF policy the same way
3. **Fixes usability**: IP literal URLs would work with strict SSRF policy (after fixing the separate bug in `requiresInspectableBrowserNavigationRedirectsForUrl`)
4. **No new dependencies**: `playwright-core` is already included

## Considerations

- **Performance**: Playwright connection has minimal overhead (cached after first use)
- **Backward compatibility**: Should not affect existing behavior for users without strict SSRF policy
- **Chrome MCP**: This proposal does not change Chrome MCP (`existing-session`) behavior

## Related Code

- `extensions/browser/src/browser/profile-capabilities.ts` - Mode detection
- `extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.tab-ops.ts` - Navigation logic
- `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts` - Playwright route handler (`gotoPageWithNavigationGuard`)

items.title: Slack sandbox attachments reject ~/.openclaw/workspaces paths resolved from /workspace media

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Summary

When OpenClaw runs in sandbox mode, Slack file sends using the documented `message send + media` flow fail for files created under `/workspace/...`.

The sandbox path is resolved to a host path under `~/.openclaw/workspaces/...`, but Slack local media validation rejects that path as not being under an allowed directory.

## Expected behavior

`message action=send + media=/workspace/...` should work for Slack in sandbox mode, just like it already does for Telegram.

## Actual behavior

Slack rejects the resolved host path with an error like:

```text
Local media path is not under an allowed directory: /home/dev/.openclaw/workspaces/general/test.md
```

## Why this looks like an upstream bug

- Telegram succeeds with the same `/workspace/...` pattern
- Slack file upload itself works once `~/.openclaw/workspaces` is added to local media roots
- A minimal local patch that adds `path.join(stateDir, "workspaces")` to default local media roots restores Slack attachments without changing the external `send + media` contract

## Reproduction

1. Enable sandbox mode with `workspaceAccess: "rw"`
2. Let an agent create `/workspace/test.md`
3. Call `message` send for Slack with `media: "/workspace/test.md"`
4. Observe the local path validation failure for `~/.openclaw/workspaces/...`

## Local workaround

Add `path.join(stateDir, "workspaces")` to default local media roots.

## Notes

This seems narrower than a general Slack upload bug. The main issue appears to be that sandbox-hosted workspace files live under `~/.openclaw/workspaces/...`, but that root is missing from Slack local media allowlists.

items.title: Opus 4.6 plain-text thinking leaks to users (not caught by thinking-tag strip)

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Description

Follow-up from #12631. The `<thinking>` tag stripping fix works correctly, but Opus 4.6 also **"thinks out loud" in plain assistant text** — no XML tags, no thinking blocks — which gets delivered directly to customers.

## Current Behavior

The model writes internal reasoning as regular text that bypasses `stripThinkingTagsFromText()`:

| What the customer received | What it actually is |
|---|---|
| *"Es Adrián, el del USB para música/DJ. Pero no tengo contexto de qué es padre."* | Agent identifying the customer internally |
| *"Sigue sin tener sentido. Le pregunto directamente:"* | Agent narrating its confusion |
| *"Ahora lo pillo — quiere saber si su padre pasa por la tienda hoy."* | Agent reasoning about intent |
| *"No tengo ficha ni historial de este número. Aviso a Efren."* | Agent narrating next action |

These are standard assistant content — no tags to strip.

## Expected Behavior

Internal reasoning should never reach the end user, regardless of whether it uses thinking tags or plain text.

## When it gets worse

At high context usage (~79%+ of the context window), the model starts ignoring system prompt instructions (including explicit "never send your thoughts" rules). Frequency increases significantly with context fill.

## Our workaround

We built a `message_sending` hook plugin as a safety net:
**→ [openclaw-thought-filter](https://github.com/Valinainformatica/openclaw-thought-filter)**

It uses regex pattern matching to catch common "thinking out loud" patterns before delivery. Works well but is a band-aid — the gateway delivery pipeline would be the proper place for this.

## Suggestion

A lightweight heuristic filter in the delivery pipeline (similar to `sanitizeUserFacingText()`) could catch these patterns at the gateway level. The `message_sending` hook point is the right place — our plugin proves the approach works.

## Related

- #12631 (closed — fixed the `<thinking>` tag leak, but not this plain-text variant)

## Configuration

- OpenClaw version: latest
- Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
- Channels: WhatsApp, Telegram

items.title: gateway restart always times out (60s) on systems without lsof

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Bug

`openclaw gateway restart` **always times out after 60 seconds** on any Linux system without `lsof` installed, even though the gateway is running and healthy.

**Severity: HIGH** — affects every restart on Arch Linux, Alpine, minimal Debian/Ubuntu, containers, and any distro that doesn't ship `lsof` by default.

## Reproduction

1. Uninstall `lsof` (or use a system that doesn't have it)
2. Run `openclaw gateway restart`
3. Observe: timeout after 60s with error:
```
Timed out after 60s waiting for gateway port 18789 to become healthy.
Port 18789 is already in use.
- Port is in use but process details are unavailable (install lsof or run as an admin user).
Port diagnostics errors: Error: spawn lsof ENOENT
Gateway restart timed out after 60s waiting for health checks.
```
4. The gateway is actually running fine — `curl http://127.0.0.1:18789/health` returns 200.

## Root Cause

In `src/cli/daemon-cli/restart-health.ts`, the `ownsPort` logic:

```typescript
const ownsPort =
  runtimePid != null
    ? portUsage.listeners.some((listener) => listenerOwnedByRuntimePid({ listener, runtimePid }))
    : gatewayListeners.length > 0 ||
      (portUsage.status === "busy" && portUsage.listeners.length === 0);
```

When `lsof` is missing, `inspectPortUsage()` returns `{ status: "busy", listeners: [] }`. The `runtimePid != null` branch calls `.some()` on an empty array → always `false` → `ownsPort = false` → `healthy = false` → loops for 120 attempts × 500ms = 60s timeout.

The `runtimePid == null` branch already has the correct fallback `(portUsage.status === "busy" && portUsage.listeners.length === 0)`, but the `runtimePid != null` branch is missing it.

## Fix

PR: #32613

items.title: Duplicate plugin loading warning when installing Feishu plugin via official install script

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Environment
- **OpenClaw Version**: 2026.3.2 (installed on March 3rd, 2026)
- **Installation Method**: Official install script (`curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`)
- **Platform**: Linux server
- **Plugin**: Feishu (installed via npm package option during setup)

## Description
After installing OpenClaw using the official installation script and configuring the Feishu channel with default npm package option, the application starts with repeated duplicate plugin warnings.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Install OpenClaw on a fresh Linux server:
   ```bash
   curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash
   ```
2. Configure Feishu channel during setup
3. Choose the default option to install the Feishu plugin as an npm package
4. Start OpenClaw gateway
5. Check the logs

## Actual Behavior
The startup log shows repeated warnings:
```
Config warnings:
- plugins.entries.feishu: plugin feishu: duplicate plugin id detected;
  later plugin may be overridden (/root/.openclaw/extensions/feishu/index.ts)
```

## Expected Behavior
The plugin should load once without warnings.

## Root Cause Analysis
The issue occurs because:
1. The Feishu plugin is installed globally via npm to `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/extensions/feishu`
2. A local copy also exists at `~/.openclaw/extensions/feishu`
3. The config file `openclaw.json` has `plugins.installs.feishu.installPath` pointing to a path that may not match the actual installation
4. This causes the plugin to be loaded twice

## Suggested Solutions

### Option 1: Installation Script Validation (Recommended)
Modify the install script to:
- Detect if a plugin is already installed globally
- Avoid creating duplicate local copies
- Set the correct `installPath` in the config file

### Option 2: Runtime Deduplication
Add plugin loading logic to:
- Detect duplicate plugin IDs during startup
- Use a priority system (e.g., config path > global > local)
- Load only the highest priority instance and log which path was chosen

### Option 3: Config Validation
Add startup validation to:
- Check if `installPath` points to an existing directory
- Auto-discover the correct path if the configured one is invalid
- Provide clear error messages with suggested fixes

## Workaround
Manually fix the configuration:
1. Remove duplicate directory: `rm -rf ~/.openclaw/extensions/feishu`
2. Update `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` to point `plugins.installs.feishu.installPath` to the global npm installation path

## Impact
This affects new users following the official installation guide, creating a poor first-run experience. The warning appears on every startup and may confuse users about which plugin instance is actually being used.

items.title: Usage dashboard shows "Avg Tokens / Msg: 0" for agents with subagent sessions

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Bug Description

The Usage dashboard's "Avg Tokens / Msg" shows `0` for agents that primarily run as subagent sessions (e.g., `worker` agent), even though individual subagent sessions correctly display token counts.

## Root Cause

Data path mismatch between token totals and message counts in the dashboard aggregation:

1. **Session store (`sessions.json`) never persists `messageCounts`** — it caches `totalTokens`, `inputTokens`, `outputTokens`, but stores `messageCounts: null`
2. **Client-side `eh()` aggregation silently skips sessions with `messageCounts: null`** — so `messages.total` stays 0
3. **`I()` (token totals) still picks up cached token values from those same sessions** — so `totalTokens` is non-zero
4. **`gh()` computes** `Math.round(totalTokens / messages.total)` → `X / 0` → displays `0`

## Expected Behavior

"Avg Tokens / Msg" should correctly reflect the average across all subagent sessions when filtering by agent.

## Observed Behavior

- Individual subagent session cards show correct token counts (e.g., 23.8K, 29.3K, 31.3K)
- Usage Overview for the same agent shows "Avg Tokens / Msg: 0" and "Across 793 messages"
- 793 messages are counted but tokens-per-message shows 0

## Suggested Fix

Either:
- **Store-side:** Persist `messageCounts` in `sessions.json` alongside token counts in `appendMessage()` / session metadata writes
- **Client-side:** Fall back to computing message counts from transcript JSONL when `messageCounts` is null, instead of silently skipping
- **Both** for correctness + performance

## Environment

- OpenClaw v2026.2.23
- Agent with subagent spawn pattern (main → worker subagents)

items.title: [Bug]: WhatsApp selfChatMode fires on all outbound DMs, not just self-chat — agent responded to third-party contacts

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Summary

When `selfChatMode: true` is set on the WhatsApp channel, the agent incorrectly processes and responds to messages sent in any DM thread — not just the user's self-chat (note-to-self).

## Config

```json
"whatsapp": {
  "enabled": true,
  "dmPolicy": "allowlist",
  "selfChatMode": true,
  "allowFrom": ["+19194576111"],
  "actions": {
    "sendMessage": true
  }
}
```

## What happened

The user was having a normal conversation with a friend (Dylan) in a WhatsApp DM. The agent started responding in that thread — treating the user's own outbound messages to Dylan as commands, and replying to Dylan directly as if he were the agent operator.

## Root cause (analysis)

The inbound log shows the sender as `+19194576111` (the user's own number) for all events — which is correct, since in WhatsApp DMs the sender field reflects who sent the message. The `allowFrom` allowlist matches, so the message passes through.

However, the agent cannot distinguish between:
1. User messaging **themselves** (self-chat / note-to-self) → should trigger the agent
2. User messaging **a third party** (e.g., a friend) → should NOT trigger the agent

The `selfChatMode` flag appears to enable agent triggering when the sender matches the user's own number, but it does not check whether the **recipient** is also the user's own number (i.e., sender == recipient == user's number).

## Expected behavior

`selfChatMode: true` should only trigger the agent when the conversation is a true self-chat: sender and recipient are the same number (the user's own WhatsApp account number). Any DM thread where the recipient is a different number should not trigger the agent, regardless of `allowFrom` rules.

## Workaround

Disabled `sendMessage` as an immediate mitigation to prevent further replies to third parties.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.3.2
- macOS: Darwin 25.3.0 arm64
- WhatsApp transport: personal account (not Business API)

labels.name: bug

items.body:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Summary

itle
sessions.json transcriptPath persisted as null — causes 400 on restart

Body
Description
All sessions in sessions.json have transcriptPath: null after gateway restart. When a session with transcriptPath: null tries to resume, it sends a broken message chain to the Anthropic API, resulting in:

HTTP 400: messages.N.content.1: unexpected `tool_use_id` found in `tool_result` blocks
or:

HTTP 400: Operation not allowed
Steps to Reproduce
Run OpenClaw v2026.2.1 with multiple agents (4 agents, ~25 sessions)
Use Anthropic API via PPIO proxy (api.ppio.com/anthropic)
After a tool call JSON parse error (model outputs malformed JSON), the session's transcript chain breaks
On gateway restart (SIGUSR1), the session cannot recover because transcriptPath is null in sessions.json
All subsequent messages to that session fail with 400
Evidence
Checked all sessions across all agents — every single session has transcriptPath: null:

# Script to check
for agent in ['main', 'sales', 'hrbp', 'bi']:
    path = f'~/.openclaw/agents/{agent}/sessions/sessions.json'
    # ALL entries show transcriptPath: None/null
Sessions that have totalTokens > 0 (active sessions with history) also have transcriptPath: null, which means the transcript file path is not being persisted.

Trigger Scenario
The chain of events that corrupts a session:

Model outputs tool_use with malformed JSON → parse error
Error is delivered to user as text reply
But the transcript still has the tool_use block without a matching tool_result
Next API call sends the broken transcript → Anthropic returns 400 (orphaned tool_use_id)
Session is permanently broken until manually deleted from sessions.json
Current Workaround
Delete the broken session key from ~/.openclaw/agents/{agent}/sessions/sessions.json and restart gateway.

Expected Behavior
transcriptPath should be correctly persisted in sessions.json
When a tool call JSON parse fails, the transcript should either:
Remove the orphaned tool_use block, or
Insert a synthetic tool_result with the error message
Environment
OpenClaw: v2026.2.1
Node: v22.22.0
OS: Ubuntu 22.04 (x64)
Model provider: PPIO proxy (Anthropic API)
Model: Claude Opus 4.6
read
View

items.title: [Feature]: Per-agent working directory (cwd) separate from workspace

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
### Summary

Support a per-agent `cwd` (or `workdir`) config key, separate from `workspace`, so multiple agents can share the same workspace files while operating in different working directories.

### Problem to solve

When running multiple agents (e.g. one per group chat), each agent needs to work in a different code directory, but they all share the same persona, memory, and configuration files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, USER.md, etc.).

Currently, `workspace` controls both the bootstrap/context files AND the default cwd for tools like `exec`. The only options are:
- Duplicate workspace files across multiple directories (maintenance burden)
- Use symlinks (fragile, platform-dependent)
- Rely on system prompt instructions to tell the agent which directory to use (works but not declarative)

### Proposed solution

Add an optional `cwd` (or `workdir`) field to `agents.list[]`:

```json5
{
  agents: {
    list: [
      {
        id: "worker-1",
        workspace: "~/.openclaw/workspace",  // shared context files
        cwd: "/home/user/projects/repo-1",   // working directory for exec/tools
      },
      {
        id: "worker-2",
        workspace: "~/.openclaw/workspace",  // same shared context
        cwd: "/home/user/projects/repo-2",   // different code directory
      },
    ],
  },
}
```

When `cwd` is set, tool operations (exec, process, read, write, edit) default to that path instead of `workspace`. Bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.) still load from `workspace`.

When `cwd` is not set, behavior stays exactly as it is today (workspace is cwd).

### Alternatives considered

- **Separate workspaces with symlinked files**: Works but fragile on Windows/WSL, extra setup per agent, symlinks can break on git operations.
- **System prompt instructions**: Currently used. Works but is not declarative, wastes tokens on routing logic, and the agent can still accidentally work in the wrong directory.
- **Sandbox binds**: Overkill when sandboxing is not needed. Adds container overhead for a simple path routing problem.

### Impact

Affected: Anyone running multiple agents that share persona/config but work on different codebases (e.g. per-group coding agents, per-project workers).
Severity: Medium - current workarounds exist but add friction.
Frequency: Every session for multi-agent setups.
Consequence: Extra config duplication, fragile symlinks, or wasted prompt tokens on directory routing instructions.

### Evidence/examples

The `agents.list[]` config already supports per-agent `workspace`, `model`, `sandbox`, and `tools` overrides. Adding `cwd` follows the same pattern.

### Additional information

Should be backward-compatible - when `cwd` is absent, current behavior is preserved.

items.title: [Bug] Slack: agent react action fails in DMs, missing messageId auto-inference, missing currentMessageTs

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Summary

Three related issues prevent the Slack `react` action from working reliably for agent-initiated emoji reactions:

### A) `slackTo` uses `user:` format for DMs — causes `channel_not_found`

In the Slack action handler, the target is computed as:

```typescript
const slackTo = isDirectMessage ? `user:${message.user}` : `channel:${message.channel}`;
```

When the agent calls `action: "react"`, the Slack API's `reactions.add` needs a **channel ID**, not a user ID. In DMs, the channel ID (the DM conversation) is available in `message.channel`, but the `user:` prefix causes the reaction to fail with `channel_not_found`.

**Fix:** Always use `channel:${message.channel}` — the Slack API resolves DM conversations correctly with the channel ID.

### B) `currentMessageTs` not exposed in `buildThreadingToolContext`

The `buildThreadingToolContext` function builds the tool context object returned to the model, but does not include `currentMessageTs`. Without it, the model has no way to know the timestamp of the message it's replying to, which is required as `messageId` for the `react` action.

**Fix:** Add `currentMessageTs: sessionCtx.MessageSid ? String(sessionCtx.MessageSid) : void 0` to both the fallback and dock return paths.

### C) `messageId` not auto-inferred for `react` action in `runMessageAction`

The `runMessageAction` function auto-infers `target` from `toolContext.currentChannelId` when not explicitly provided, but does **not** auto-infer `messageId` from `toolContext.currentMessageTs` for the `react` action. This means models must manually specify `messageId` for every reaction, which they often fail to do since the message timestamp is not obvious.

**Fix:** After the target inference block, add:

```typescript
if ((action === "react" || action === "reactions") && !params.messageId) {
    const inferredMsgId = input.toolContext?.currentMessageTs?.trim();
    if (inferredMsgId) params.messageId = inferredMsgId;
}
```

### Impact

These three issues together make agent-initiated Slack reactions unreliable:
- (A) breaks reactions in DMs entirely
- (B) makes the model blind to what message it's reacting to
- (C) requires the model to manually extract and pass timestamps

### Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.2.21-2
- Slack Socket Mode
- Multiple agents, multi-workspace setup

### Workaround

We have a combined idempotent patch script that fixes all three in the dist bundle. Happy to submit a source-level PR if maintainers prefer.

[AI-assisted]

items.body:
## Description

Discord WebSocket connections silently die when AI message processing exceeds ~30 seconds. The health-monitor detects the stuck state and restarts the connection, but this introduces several minutes of downtime.

This is the same issue reported in #10743, which was closed by the stale bot without any code fix or human engagement.

## Log Evidence

```
warn discord/monitor  Slow listener detected: DiscordMessageListener MESSAGE_CREATE 64.2 seconds
warn discord/monitor  Slow listener detected: DiscordMessageListener MESSAGE_CREATE 31.4 seconds
info gateway/health-monitor  [discord:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
```

## Reproduction

Trigger any AI response that takes >30s to process (e.g., a model with extended thinking). The WebSocket connection drops silently and the health-monitor restarts.

A standalone WebSocket test connecting to `wss://gateway.discord.gg` with proper heartbeat ran 10 minutes with zero drops on the same machine and network, confirming this is not a network-level issue. The problem is that long-running message processing blocks the gateway event loop, causing missed heartbeats.

## Additional Notes

- The health-monitor stuck threshold and related parameters are hardcoded constants with no user-facing configuration to tune them.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.3.1
- macOS ARM64
- Node.js v25.6.1

items.body:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Summary

In OpenClaw 2026.3.1, the compiled gateway bundle reorders handlePluginRequest after canvasHost, causing 405 for plugin HTTP endpoints. The TypeScript source has the correct ordering, but the bundler reorders them in the compiled output.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Install `openclaw@2026.3.1` globally
2. 2. Configure a Google Chat channel (or any plugin that registers an HTTP handler via `api.registerHttpHandler`)
3. 3. Send a POST request to the plugin's HTTP endpoint (e.g., `POST /googlechat`)
4. 4. Observe `405 Method Not Allowed` instead of the expected plugin response

### Expected behavior

`handlePluginRequest` should run before `canvasHost.handleHttpRequest`, matching the source code order. Plugin HTTP handlers should be able to process POST requests.

### Actual behavior

`canvasHost.handleHttpRequest` intercepts the request first and returns 405 for non-GET/HEAD methods. The plugin handler never executes.

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.1

### Operating system

Debian Linux (remote server)

### Install method

npm install -g openclaw

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
## Workaround

Manually patch the compiled bundle to insert `handlePluginRequest` before the canvas host block:


// Insert before: if (await handleA2uiHttpRequest(req, res)) return;
if (handlePluginRequest && await handlePluginRequest(req, res)) return;
```

items.title: Telegram: Support real-time streaming responses (editMessageText)

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Feature Request: Real-Time Streaming Responses for Telegram

### Context
Telegram just announced native support for bots streaming responses in real-time ([Durov announcement](https://x.com/durov/status/2028455440862830970)). Text appears word-by-word like ChatGPT instead of waiting for the full reply to complete.

### Current Behavior
OpenClaw buffers the full LLM response, then sends it as a single message to Telegram. For long responses this means the user waits 10-30+ seconds staring at "typing..." before seeing anything.

### Proposed Behavior
When the Telegram channel plugin detects a streaming response from the model:

1. **Send initial message** once the first N tokens (or first sentence) arrive
2. **Progressively `editMessageText`** as more tokens stream in (throttled to avoid rate limits — e.g. every 500ms or every ~50 tokens)
3. **Final edit** when the response is complete (clean up any formatting artifacts)
4. **Graceful fallback** — if editing fails (rate limit, message too old), fall back to current behavior

### Technical Notes
- OpenClaw already streams tokens internally from the LLM provider
- The Telegram Bot API `editMessageText` method is the mechanism
- Rate limiting: Telegram allows ~30 edits/sec globally, but should throttle to ~2-3 edits/sec per message to be safe
- Consider a config flag: `channels.telegram.streaming: true/false` (opt-in)
- Markdown formatting may need special handling during streaming (partial markdown can break rendering)
- Tool calls / thinking blocks should probably not stream (only the final text output)
